The Fluorescence of the Lamanites

Elder Spencer W. Kimball
Of the Council of the Twelve

[Address Delivered at the Annual General Relief Society Conference, October 1, 1952]

I pay tribute to you, the mothers of Israel, and thank the Lord for you, and for the glorious work that you are doing in Zion.

I come to realize more and more, as my experience broadens, the vast influence and power that a small minority may wield in this world, in politics, in religion, in social activities, everywhere you go. A small group, united in purpose, with definite goals, may greatly influence the great majorities.

Sister Spafford mentioned that the subject of my talk would be the Indian Relations work. I should like to broaden that slightly, and discuss with you the minority program.

Last year the brethren saw fit to reorganize the proselyting program within the stakes of Zion. The First Presidency and the Council of the Twelve were brought into the program with the First Council of the Seventy, and some additional prestige has come to this great program. The 35,000-odd conversions in the past fifteen years under the First Council of the Seventy have proved the power of this work.

I feel that we have hardly scratched the surface in the proselyting endeavor in the stakes of Zion. Now that stakes are scattered from ocean to ocean, from the north to the south, and we have strong leadership and many people, there is no reason now why we cannot reach out and bring into our numbers the millions of good, honorable people of the world, who are searching for the truth.

The stake missionary program, as now re-organized, is under the direction of President Stephen L Richards of the First Presidency. President Joseph Fielding Smith is the chairman of all of the missionary endeavor. There is a sub-committee on the stake missionary work, and included in the stake missionary work is the proselyting of the minority groups.

This is not limited to the Indians, though in many of the stakes, perhaps half of the stakes of Zion, the Indian program looms the largest of all the minority groups. However, we have far more Lamanite people speaking the Spanish language in the stakes of Zion, and there are areas where the Japanese are predominant, and there are other minority groups—the Greeks, the Italians, and others.

It is the purpose of the stake missionary work to reach out and proselyte, and bring into the Church these many wonderf
